docs.title: Virtual Barrels: A Quant's Tour of the Oil Market
docs.subtitle: Real options, spreads, volatility risk premium, and spread-option pricing — the mental models that turn physical crude into a tradeable portfolio.
docs.body: 1. The Real-Options Lens on Oil Most introductions to crude oil begin with a map: the basins, the chokepoints, the swing producers. Bouchouev starts somewhere else. His opening move in Virtual Barrels is to argue that every physical asset in the oil chain is really a portfolio of options written on prices the trader can already see in the futures market. Once you accept that translation, the rest of the book follows as a sequence of valuation problems rather than a parade of geopolitical anecdotes. The cleanest case is the producing well. A reservoir is not an obligation to pump; it is the right to pump, period by period, if the prevailing price P exceeds the lifting cost K. That is a call option — max(P - K, 0) — repeated along the field's economic life. The book is explicit that this reframing does more than decorate a DCF: the producer's flexibility to defer extraction when prices collapse has quantifiable value beyond net present value, and that value rises with volatility, with time to expiry, and with the risk-free rate. The same structure reappears everywhere. Pipelines and tankers hold location-spread options, profitable to exercise when the Gulf–Rotterdam or Cushing–LLS differential exceeds the cost of moving a barrel. Storage tanks hold calendar-spread options: fill when the forward price less carry exceeds prompt, empty when the curve inverts. A refinery is a bundle of crack-spread options — gasoline and distillate against crude, net of processing costs — that the operator runs harder or softer depending on which outputs are in the money. Nothing in the physical plant changes; the trader simply relabels each asset by its payoff. Three real options that make up the oil chain. Axes and payoff levels are schematic; the point is the functional form, not the magnitudes. For this lens to work, "price" has to mean something precise, and here Bouchouev inverts a textbook assumption. In most derivatives courses the futures contract is a derivative of the spot. In oil, he argues, the relationship runs the other way: because there is no universal, continuously-clearing spot market for a barrel of crude, physical deals are struck at the prompt futures price plus or minus a basis for location, grade and timing. The nearest-to-expire contract is the anchor; the "spot" is a derived quantity. That is why an option-on-production is really written on the front of the futures curve, and why the shape of that curve — contango versus backwardation — determines whether storage is a live option or a dead one. The last piece is why these options are worth anything at all. The modern oil ecosystem is, as the book puts it, just-in-time: inventories, shipping, refining runs and even spare capacity have been optimised to a thin margin. That optimisation is precisely what makes the system unstable. A hurricane, a pipeline outage, an OPEC surprise — any of them can drive prices through strike levels that looked safely out of the money a week earlier. In option-pric

docs.title: Virtual Barrels — Chapter Notes
docs.subtitle: Full chapter-by-chapter reference extracted verbatim from the library's Virtual Barrels chapter summaries.
docs.body: 1. Chapter 1: Introduction to Real Options and Oil Market Dynamics Bouchouev opens Virtual Barrels by reframing the entire oil complex through a single, unifying lens: real options. A producer sitting on a reservoir, a pipeline operator weighing whether to dispatch a tanker, a storage tank with empty capacity, a refiner deciding how hard to run its crackers — each is holding a right without an obligation, and each therefore owns something whose value exceeds the simple arithmetic of expected cash flows. Flexibility, Bouchouev argues, is itself an asset. It has a price, it is sensitive to volatility, and it can be analysed with the same machinery that prices financial options. From this foundation the chapter builds outwards. Production becomes a call on the future price of oil; transport becomes an option on a location spread; storage is an option on a time spread; refining is a spread option on the crack between product and crude. Hedging ties the structure together, letting participants lock in, floor, or cap the cash flows that those operational options throw off. Woven through the exposition is a more subversive idea — that oil, by virtue of its scale, indispensability, and deep derivatives market, functions as a form of money, complete with its own Keynesian own rate of interest that captures the trade-off between consuming a barrel today and storing it for tomorrow. Alongside the mathematics, Bouchouev insists on the role of mental models — the simplified, biased, adaptive heuristics traders carry in their heads. These models drive behaviour even when they are wrong, which is precisely why the oil market's textbook certainties tend to break. Chief among those broken certainties is the direction of price discovery itself: in oil, the spot price is usually derived from the nearest-to-expire futures contract plus a basis, not the other way round. The chapter closes on the fragility of a just-in-time energy system whose lack of slack guarantees volatility, and on the growing cast of new entrants — inflation hedgers, quant funds, option specialists — whose presence has reshaped how risk is priced, transferred, and exploited. Figure 1.1 — The oil value chain as a sequence of real options. Each stage carries an exercise rule; the surrounding just-in-time ecosystem amplifies shocks and raises the premium on flexibility itself. Key Technical Concepts 💡 Real Options A real option represents the right, but not the obligation, to undertake a specific business decision, often involving capital investment, at a future point in time. This concept draws heavily from financial options theory but applies it to tangible assets and operational decisions within a business context. Unlike their financial counterparts, real options are typically not traded on exchanges. Their value derives from the flexibility they offer, allowing businesses to adapt to evolving market conditions. For instance, an oil producer possesses a real option to extract oil from a reservo

docs.title: Python for Algorithmic Trading Cookbook — Chapter Notes
docs.subtitle: Full chapter-by-chapter reference synthesising the library's Python for Algorithmic Trading Cookbook chapter summaries.
docs.body: 1. Acquiring and Analyzing Financial Market Data with Python Before a single backtest can run, before a single signal can fire, there is the unglamorous but decisive problem of getting the data right. Chapter 1 of Python for Algorithmic Trading Cookbook treats data acquisition as a first-class engineering discipline rather than a preamble. It begins with the premise that every downstream claim — Sharpe ratios, alpha estimates, factor exposures, option Greeks — is only as defensible as the pipeline that fed it. The chapter therefore establishes a working toolkit: pandas as the substrate, pandasdatareader as the direct conduit to well-known providers, and the OpenBB Platform as a unified abstraction layer over dozens of commercial and open APIs. The conceptual arc moves from the mechanics of retrieval to the structures that hold the data and finally to the economics that give it meaning. You learn how to install providers, authenticate with API keys, pull historical equity prices, and resample them against a DatetimeIndex. You then encounter the richer shapes of modern markets — option chains with multi-level indices across expiry and strike, futures curves stitched from contracts of different maturities, and Fama-French factor files that reframe returns in terms of size, value, and market premia. The chapter deliberately intertwines these topics because they share a common failure mode: mis-aligned indices, stale adjustments, and silent provider changes that corrupt reproducibility. Interwoven with the mechanics is a quieter argument about epistemic discipline. The chapter insists that data quality, timeliness, reproducibility, and transparency are axioms, not afterthoughts. It surfaces the Efficient Market Hypothesis as the null your strategies must beat, and the Fama-French three-factor model as one historically durable way they have. It gestures at Black-Scholes and the Greeks not as formulas to memorise but as sensitivities that give structure to the chaos of option prices. By the end, you should be able to stand up a clean local dataset of equities, options, and factors, reason about its provenance, and prepare it for backtests, predictive models, portfolio optimisation, and risk management — the four applied threads that the rest of the book will pull on. Synthetic year-long index of SPY against Fama-French factors (SMB, HML, Rm-Rf) — illustrating how factor data reframes returns away from a single-market lens. Key Technical Concepts 💡 Data Acquisition The process of obtaining financial market data from various sources is fundamental to algorithmic trading and quantitative analysis. This involves understanding different data providers, their APIs, data formats (CSV, JSON), and potential data limitations. Data quality, timeliness, and consistency are crucial considerations. For example, using the OpenBB platform provides a streamlined approach to accessing multiple data providers, while pandasdatareader offers direct integration with pandas D

docs.title: Forecasting: Principles and Practice — Book Notes
docs.subtitle: Full book notes synthesising every chapter of Hyndman & Athanasopoulos' Forecasting: Principles and Practice.
docs.body: 1. Forecasting: Principles and Practice — Full Book Notes Hyndman and Athanasopoulos's Forecasting: Principles and Practice (2nd ed., 2018) is the definitive applied textbook for time series forecasting, grounded in the R fpp2 ecosystem (forecast v8.3 + ggplot2). The book opens with a philosophical foundation — predictability depends on understanding of contributing factors, data availability, and whether forecasts themselves influence outcomes — and progresses methodically through visualization, benchmark methods, transformations, residual diagnostics, regression, decomposition, exponential smoothing (ETS), ARIMA, dynamic regression, hierarchical reconciliation, and advanced methods including TBATS, VAR, and neural network autoregression. The probabilistic framing is central: every forecast is a summary of a probability distribution over a future random variable, and prediction intervals (typically 80% and 95%) communicate uncertainty. The notation y{T+h|T} for h-step-ahead forecasts made at time T recurs throughout. The book builds two dominant model families — ETS (18 innovations state space models classified by Error × Trend × Seasonal components, selected via AICc through ets()) and ARIMA (built from the backshift operator B, stationarity enforced via differencing, fit through the Hyndman-Khandakar algorithm in auto.arima()). Though linear ETS models are special cases of ARIMA, the two classes overlap only partially and AICc cannot compare across them; out-of-sample evaluation via tsCV() is required. Later chapters move beyond univariate modelling: dynamic regression marries regression predictors with ARIMA errors (estimated by minimising ARIMA errors, not regression errors); hierarchical reconciliation uses the summing matrix S and the MinT estimator to produce coherent forecasts across aggregation levels; and advanced methods handle complex seasonality (mstl, dynamic harmonic regression, TBATS), multivariate dynamics (VAR), and nonlinearity (NNAR). The closing chapter addresses the messy pragmatics — weekly/daily seasonality, count data via Croston, constrained forecasts through scaled logit, missing-value imputation, and crucially forecast combination, where simple averaging of diverse methods consistently beats the best individual model. The book's deepest lesson is that no single method dominates; disciplined diagnostics, information-criterion-based selection, and ensemble combination are the backbone of reliable applied forecasting. const Ch1Vis1 = () => ( <svg viewBox="0 0 860 460" style={{width:'100%',height:'auto',background:C.bg,borderRadius:8}}> <defs> <marker id="arr" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="9" refY="5" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" orient="auto"> <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill= /> </marker> </defs> <text x="430" y="28" textAnchor="middle" fill= fontSize="15" fontFamily=>Forecasting Method Family Tree</text> <text x="430" y="46" textAnchor="middle" fill= fontSize="11" fontFamily=>Hyndman and Athanasopoulos (2018)</text> <

docs.title: Crude Oil Handbook — Chapter Notes
docs.subtitle: Full chapter-by-chapter reference synthesising the library's Crude Oil Handbook chapter summaries.
docs.body: 1. Technical Analysis of Global Crude Oil Markets: Pricing, Logistics, and Market Dynamics The global crude oil market is one of the most consequential, liquid, and technically layered commodity systems in the world economy, and this opening chapter lays down the structural grammar needed to read it. What began in the twentieth century as a tightly administered industry — with official selling prices set by producing-country governments and national oil companies, and Free on Board pricing that reflected strategic rather than economic considerations — has migrated, over the last several decades, into a market-driven architecture in which spot cargoes, benchmark crudes, futures contracts, and formula-linked term contracts continuously negotiate against each other. That migration is not cosmetic. It changes who bears volatility, how information propagates from a loading port to a refinery gate, and which institutions matter. The spot market, though smaller in physical volume than the term-contract book, has become the price-discovery organ of the entire system: a handful of benchmark crudes — Brent Blend, West Texas Intermediate, Dubai — act as reference points whose averaged quotations flow into formulas used by OPEC members, independents, and refiners around the world. Term contracts still provide security of supply and of offtake, but their prices breathe in time with spot. Futures and options sit above this, allowing participants to hedge price exposure or take speculative views on a time structure that can shift from contango to backwardation within weeks. Running parallel to pricing is the physical reality the market never escapes: crude oil is not a homogenous fluid. API gravity, sulfur content, viscosity, trace metals and residue yields all differ between grades, and those differences flow directly into refinery economics via netback pricing, which subtracts freight, refining, and marketing costs from refined-product revenues to infer what a specific crude is worth to a specific refiner on a specific day. Logistics — VLCCs and Suezmaxes, pipeline systems, storage at Cushing and Rotterdam — are not background scenery; they are part of the price. Understanding how pricing mechanisms, quality, and logistics interlock is the prerequisite for every later chapter, and the purpose of this one. Five decades of pricing evolution: administered OSPs gave way to spot-based benchmarks, then to formula pricing that feeds the modern term contract. Key Technical Concepts 💡 Crude Oil Pricing Mechanisms The evolution of crude oil pricing from administered systems to market-driven mechanisms represents a fundamental shift in the industry. Historically, governments and national oil companies controlled prices through official selling prices (OSPs) and Free on Board (FOB) pricing, often reflecting political and strategic considerations rather than pure market forces. This system offered a degree of stability but lacked responsiveness to real-time supply and dem

docs.title: Bayesian Analysis with Python — Chapter Notes
docs.subtitle: Full chapter-by-chapter reference synthesising the library's Bayesian Analysis with Python (Third Edition) chapter summaries.
docs.body: 1. Chapter 1: Thinking Probabilistically - A Foundation for Bayesian Data Analysis Chapter 1 establishes the conceptual ground on which the rest of Martin's book is built: a reframing of probability itself. Where a frequentist reads a probability as the long-run relative frequency of an event under hypothetical repetition, a Bayesian reads it as a quantified degree of belief that can be revised in light of data. This seemingly philosophical shift has sharp practical consequences. It permits prior knowledge to enter the analysis formally, it produces posterior distributions that describe what we now believe about parameters, and it replaces the ritual of p-values and confidence intervals with statements of the form 'given the data, there is a 95% probability the parameter lies in this interval'. The engine of this reframing is Bayes' theorem, stated here as P(H|E) = [P(E|H) P(H)] / P(E). Each term is given an operational role: P(H) is the prior, P(E|H) is the likelihood that connects parameters to observed data, P(H|E) is the posterior, and P(E) is the marginal evidence that acts as a normalising constant. The chapter walks through this update using accessible examples — the probability a patient has a disease given a positive test, the probability of heads in a sequence of coin flips — to make the arithmetic feel less abstract than the axioms. Martin then traces the historical arc. Thomas Bayes' 18th-century essay introduced inverse probability; Pierre-Simon Laplace sharpened and applied it; yet Bayesian methods were practically confined to conjugate models (where the posterior shares the functional form of the prior) until the late 20th century. The arrival of Markov Chain Monte Carlo — Metropolis-Hastings, later Hamiltonian Monte Carlo — broke that ceiling, allowing samples from arbitrarily complex posteriors. Probabilistic programming languages such as PyMC sit on top of MCMC and let the analyst declare the model (priors, likelihood, structure) and hand inference to the machine. The chapter closes its opening argument with a pragmatist's point: Bayesian analysis gives a complete picture of uncertainty. Rather than a point estimate plus a standard error, it delivers a distribution over parameters, which propagates naturally through predictions, risk calculations, and hierarchical structures. The remainder of the book will turn these foundations into usable PyMC code, but Chapter 1's task is to make the reader think probabilistically — to see every unknown as a random variable whose distribution is the object of interest. Bayes' update visualised: a diffuse prior (blue) is multiplied pointwise by the likelihood of the observed data (amber), then renormalised to give the posterior (green). The posterior is tighter and shifted toward regions the data supports — the core mechanic of Bayesian inference. Key Technical Concepts 💡 Bayesian vs. Frequentist Statistics Bayesian statistics fundamentally differs from frequentist statistics in its interpreta

docs.title: Applied Bayesian Statistics — Paper Notes
docs.subtitle: Synthesised summary of the Applied Bayesian Statistics paper preserving every Key Technical Concept verbatim.
docs.body: 1. Applied Bayesian Statistics: Foundational Framework Christopher Sims's Applied Bayesian Statistics, delivered as a 2007 summer seminar at Princeton, makes a sustained case that Bayesian reasoning should be the default mental model for econometric inference — not merely another technical toolkit competing with frequentist methods. The paper's distinctive voice is candid about its intent: many of its claims are obvious to Bayesian practitioners and outrageous to frequentist econometricians, which is precisely why a seminar setting is the right venue for restating arguments typically excluded from journal articles. The core philosophical move is to relocate the conditioning set for probability statements. Frequentist inference offers only pre-sample assertions — a 95% confidence interval contains the true parameter with probability 0.95 before the data are observed; afterwards that probability collapses to zero or one. Yet practitioners and decision makers universally read intervals as post-sample uncertainty statements, which they can only be if they approximate posterior probability intervals. Bayesian inference directly delivers what users actually want: P(theta | data), a characterisation of parameter uncertainty conditional on the sample that has actually been observed. Sims systematically dismantles the standard objections. The supposed subjectivity of Bayesian inference is overstated: the rules transforming prior into posterior are pure mathematics, and frequentist procedures embed their own subjective choices — in model specification, test selection, bandwidth, and interpretation — merely less transparently. The supposed computational difficulty is overstated: MCMC has removed the practical barrier for most problems, and where thinking about priors is genuinely hard, that difficulty is shared by principled frequentist derivation. The supposed weakness of frequentist nonparametric assumptions is illusory in finite samples: kernel estimators require implicit restrictions on function variability just as real as any prior, while asymptotic guarantees under weak assumptions simply do not license small-sample assertions under weak assumptions. The technical spine of the argument rests on asymptotic equivalence. Under standard regularity conditions, if a scaled-and-centered frequentist estimator converges in distribution to a Gaussian with parameter-free covariance, the Bayesian posterior of the scaled-and-centered parameter converges to the same Gaussian. Frequentist confidence statements become interpretable as limited-information approximate Bayesian probability statements — conditioning on the estimator rather than the full sample. The practical upshot: adopting Bayesian thinking costs nothing in large-sample validity while gaining transparency, decision-theoretic coherence, and the honest admission of the assumptions frequentist methods make implicitly. This chapter introduces the foundational framework; subsequent chapters show it working

docs.title: Advances in Financial Machine Learning — Chapter Notes
docs.subtitle: Full chapter-by-chapter reference synthesising Marcos López de Prado's Advances in Financial Machine Learning chapter summaries.
docs.body: 1. Foundational Principles of Financial Machine Learning Marcos López de Prado opens "Advances in Financial Machine Learning" with a claim that is at once modest and radical: that financial machine learning (FML) is not, and cannot be, a routine transplant of general-purpose ML techniques onto financial data. Markets are not static image libraries or clean text corpora. They are adversarial, low signal-to-noise, non-stationary systems with reflexive feedback — any edge discovered becomes, upon discovery, an input to the price process and therefore partly self-annihilating. Generic algorithms, applied without adaptation, fail in this environment in ways that are subtle, statistically invisible in-sample, and financially catastrophic out-of-sample. The chapter's central argument is therefore structural rather than algorithmic. López de Prado contrasts two organisational paradigms for FML research. The first, which he names the Sisyphus Paradigm, hands a single analyst the end-to-end task of conceiving, building, testing and deploying a strategy. This mirrors the cottage-industry origins of quantitative finance but scales badly: it fosters overfitting, duplicates effort across a firm, destroys institutional memory with each resignation, and produces strategies that are statistically indistinguishable from the kind of lucky curve-fits one would expect from running thousands of uncoordinated experiments on the same data. The alternative — the Meta-Strategy Paradigm — borrows its metaphor from modern industrial mining. When the easily visible gold nuggets (macroscopic alpha) are gone, the remaining value lies in microscopic particles that require specialised, collaborative extraction: geologists, chemists, engineers, metallurgists. FML, by analogy, should be organised as a production chain of specialists — data curators, feature engineers, strategists, backtesters, portfolio constructors, execution engineers — each contributing to a shared artefact under a common scientific discipline. This framing is not decorative; it governs the structure of the entire book. Against this organisational backdrop the chapter surveys the epistemic hazards the rest of the volume will confront in depth: overfitting, multiple testing, selection bias, data snooping, the difference between systematic and discretionary strategies, the specific pathologies of different backtesting paradigms, and the quiet ethical problem that overfit strategies — even when deployed in good faith — transmit unrealistic expectations to allocators and beneficiaries. The opening chapter's purpose is to install the vocabulary and the scientific attitude; the mechanics follow. Figure 1.1 — Stylised load distribution across the Meta-Strategy production chain. Each station owns a distinct failure mode; coupling them in a single quant's head (the Sisyphus Paradigm) concentrates the full stack of risks onto one untested decision-maker. Key Technical Concepts 💡 Financial Machine Learning as a Distinct S

docs.title: 40 Classic Crude Oil Trades — Chapter Notes
docs.subtitle: Full chapter-by-chapter reference synthesising the library's 40 Classic Crude Oil Trades chapter summaries.
docs.body: 1. Chapter 1: Advanced Crude Oil Trading Strategies: Arbitrage, Market Dynamics, and Geopolitical Influences Crude oil, the lifeblood of the global economy, moves through an extraordinarily intricate network of buyers, sellers, transporters, and financial intermediaries — and every barrel that reaches a refinery carries with it a long lineage of strategic decisions, logistical calculations, and risk transfers. This opening chapter of 40 Classic Crude Oil Trades frames the commodity not as a passive cargo but as the active nexus where economics, finance, and geopolitics collide. Trading crude is never merely a matter of ownership transfer: it is a discipline of managing price risk, navigating physical constraints, and capitalising on the dynamic fluctuations that roll through the market each day. The strategies traders deploy span an enormous spectrum. At one end are short-term speculative plays reacting to breaking news or intraday technical signals; at the other sit long-term hedging programmes that an airline or refiner might maintain for years. In between lie the bread-and-butter approaches examined throughout the book: arbitrage between geographies and grades, logistics and storage plays driven by pipeline capacity and tank availability, and geopolitical trading that profits from the price dislocations of sanctions, conflicts, and diplomatic pivots. Every one of these strategies requires the trader to read a complex interplay of fundamentals, technical patterns, and political signals simultaneously. Two structural tensions define the market. The first is the constant dialogue between physical and financial trading: physical cargoes of Brent or WTI settle against futures prices, and futures prices in turn respond to what is happening at loading terminals, in pipelines, and in refinery feedstock tanks. The second is the role of benchmarks — Brent priced in the North Sea and WTI priced at Cushing, Oklahoma — which provide standardised reference points for a significant portion of global trading. Understanding why a pipeline outage in the Permian shows up in WTI-Brent spreads within hours, or why European refinery demand lifts dated Brent against its forward curve, is foundational to everything that follows. This chapter therefore serves as both a primer and a map. It introduces the theoretical scaffolding drawn from economics, finance, and strategic analysis, and situates these concepts inside the practical reality of traders who must act in minutes rather than months. By its end, the reader has the vocabulary to dissect the forty case studies that follow — each a concrete illustration of how arbitrage, market dynamics, and geopolitical influence combined to produce an exemplary profit or instructive loss. How fundamentals drive price discovery on Brent and WTI benchmarks, with geographical, calendar, and quality arbitrage overlaid, and the diverse participants who interact with the resulting physical and financial flows. Key Technical Concepts

docs.title: Ts Feature Engineering Guide
docs.subtitle: Lags, rolling statistics, Fourier transforms, calendar features, and the leakage traps that invalidate everything. The systematic approach to turning temporal data into features that XGBoost, LightGBM, and EBMs can co...
docs.body: 1. Why Feature Engineering Is the Forecast Your LightGBM model doesn't know it's forecasting a time series. It sees rows and columns — a tabular dataset like any other. The temporal intelligence — trend, seasonality, autocorrelation, external drivers — lives entirely in the features you engineer. If you create good lag features, the model learns from recent history. If you create good rolling statistics, it captures trend and volatility. If you create good calendar features, it captures seasonality. If you create bad features, or leak future information into them, no model and no hyperparameter tuning can save you. This is the article that the Shapash time series guide assumed you'd already read. Every SHAP explanation, every Shapash dashboard, every EBM shape function explains features, not the underlying time series. "lag1 contributed +$18k" is only meaningful if lag1 was correctly engineered — shifted to prevent leakage, named for human readability, and grouped with related lags for stakeholder presentation. Feature engineering quality determines both forecast accuracy and explanation quality. This article covers every feature family systematically: lags, rolling statistics, calendar, Fourier, differencing, exogenous regressors, and interactions. It covers the leakage traps that invalidate pipelines. It covers automated extraction with tsfresh and featuretools. And it covers the connection to explainability — why your feature choices determine what SHAP and Shapash can tell you. Every concept section contains a runnable notebook cell. 💡 Notebook Map Cells 0–9: Cell 0 Setup (§1) · Cell 1 Lags (§3) · Cell 2 Rolling Stats (§4) · Cell 3 Calendar + Fourier (§5–6) · Cell 4 Exogenous + Diff (§7–8) · Cell 5 Interaction Features (§9) · Cell 6 Train Model (§11) · Cell 7 PFI Feature Selection (§12) · Cell 8 Leakage Audit (§10) · Cell 9 Explainability Labels (§13) 2. The Core Transformation The fundamental idea: each row in the tabular dataset represents one forecast origin — the point in time from which you make a prediction. Each column represents one engineered feature that captures temporal information available at that origin. The target column is the value you're predicting (demand at time t+h, where h is the forecast horizon). This framing converts any time series problem into a supervised regression problem that any tabular model can solve. Raw time series (left) becomes a tabular dataset (right) where each row is a forecast origin and each column is an engineered feature. The model sees only rows and columns — the temporal intelligence lives entirely in the features. The forecast origin concept is critical for avoiding leakage. At each origin time t, you can only use information available up to time t (for a next-step forecast h=1) or up to time t−h+1 (for a direct multi-step forecast of horizon h). Any feature that incorporates information from time t+1 or later is leaking future data. This constraint determines which lags, rolling windows, and

docs.title: Stochastic Dp Encyclopaedic Article
docs.subtitle: In 1953, a RAND mathematician wrote an equation that became the heart of sequential decision-making — from inventory warehouses to AlphaGo. Seven decades later, the world is still learning how to solve it.
docs.body: Richard Bellman was hiding. Not from an enemy — from a budget committee. It was 1952, and the applied mathematician at the RAND Corporation in Santa Monica, California, was developing a mathematical framework for multi-stage decision processes that he needed to shield from Charles Erwin Wilson, the Secretary of Defense, who had, in Bellman's later telling, "a pathological fear and hatred of the term research." The solution was a name. "I thought dynamic programming was a good name," Bellman wrote in his autobiography. "It was something not even a Congressman could object to." The name stuck. The mathematics it concealed would reshape operations research, control theory, economics, and — half a century later — artificial intelligence. Dynamic programming, in its stochastic form, is the mathematical framework for making optimal sequential decisions when the consequences of those decisions are uncertain. The core insight is Bellman's Principle of Optimality: "An optimal policy has the property that whatever the initial state and initial decision are, the remaining decisions must constitute an optimal policy with regard to the state resulting from the first decision." This recursive structure — the idea that the optimal solution to a large problem can be decomposed into optimal solutions to smaller subproblems — gives rise to the Bellman equation, a functional equation that relates the value of being in a state today to the values of the states you might reach tomorrow, weighted by the probabilities of reaching them and discounted by how much you care about the future. "An optimal policy has the property that whatever the initial state and initial decision are, the remaining decisions must constitute an optimal policy with regard to the state resulting from the first decision." The equation is elegant, powerful, and — for any problem of realistic scale — computationally intractable. Bellman himself coined the term "curse of dimensionality" to describe the fundamental obstacle: the number of states in a dynamic programming problem grows exponentially with the number of state variables. A problem with ten state variables, each discretised into a hundred levels, requires evaluating 10^20^ states — more than the number of grains of sand on Earth. The entire history of stochastic dynamic programming, from Bellman's 1953 RAND report to the deep reinforcement learning systems that defeated human champions at Go and StarCraft in the 2010s and 2020s, can be read as a seventy-year war against this curse. The RAND Corporation headquarters in Santa Monica, California, where Richard Bellman developed dynamic programming in the early 1950s. The institution, founded in 1948 as a Cold War think tank, housed mathematicians, economists, and game theorists whose work would define operations research for decades. Bellman was born in Brooklyn in 1920, studied mathematics at Brooklyn College, earned an MA from the University of Wisconsin, worked at Los Alamos during World

docs.title: Splitwise Python Article
docs.subtitle: From OAuth handshakes to debt-minimisation graphs — everything a data engineer needs to wire Python into Splitwise's REST API.
docs.body: 01. What is Splitwise? — s1 02. The Python SDK Landscape — s2 03. Authentication Deep Dive — s3 04. Core Object Model — s4 05. Reading Data — s5 06. Writing Data — s6 07. Analytics & Reporting — s7 08. Rate Limits & Best Practices — s8 09. Real-World Use Cases — s9 10. API Reference Cheatsheet — s10 Splitwise in Python: The Complete Practitioner's Guide From OAuth handshakes to debt-minimisation graphs — everything a data engineer, automation developer, or financial-analysis practitioner needs to wire Python directly into Splitwise's REST API using the splitwise SDK. What Is Splitwise? Splitwise is a debt-tracking and expense-splitting platform launched in 2011, now serving tens of millions of users globally. Unlike a payment processor, it is a ledger of obligations — it records who paid what, computes who owes whom, and optimises the resulting debt graph to minimise the number of settlement transactions required. At its core, Splitwise solves a surprisingly hard combinatorial problem: given N people with M expenses between them, find the minimum set of payments that resolves every debt. Naively, this is O(N²) bilateral settlements; Splitwise's "Simplify Debts" feature reduces this to at most N-1 transfers by treating the problem as a flow network. The platform exposes a full REST API at https://secure.splitwise.com/api/v3.0/, supporting OAuth 1.0a, OAuth 2.0, and personal API keys. Every resource — users, groups, expenses, comments, currencies, categories — is accessible programmatically. This makes Splitwise an attractive target for financial automation, household budget analysis, and shared-economy tooling. 💡 Key Concept Expenses not assigned to a group are placed in a virtual group with id=0. Always filter getExpenses() results by getPayment() == False to exclude settlement transactions from spending analyses. The Python SDK Landscape Several community SDKs exist across languages for the Splitwise API. For Python, the canonical choice is namaggarwal/splitwise, a 208-star MIT-licensed package on PyPI that provides complete typed object wrappers around every API endpoint. It is the only Python SDK listed on the official Splitwise developer documentation. The SDK supports Python 3.7 through 3.11 and runs on PyPy. It wraps every API resource as a Python class with explicit getter and setter methods — a deliberate design choice that provides IDE autocomplete at the cost of more verbose code than a simple dict-based client. Authentication Deep Dive Splitwise supports three authentication mechanisms. Choosing the wrong one for your use case is the most common source of integration friction. Understanding the tradeoffs upfront saves hours of debugging. For personal automation — monthly spending reports, household budget dashboards, CLI tools — the API key is the fastest path. Register an application at https://secure.splitwise.com/oauthclients, then use the API key you receive directly. This method acts on your account only and is not suitable for mu

docs.title: Shapash Timeseries Technical Article
docs.subtitle: How to use MAIF's explainability library to make lag features, rolling statistics, and calendar effects interpretable to business stakeholders — from feature engineering to production deployment.
docs.body: You've built a time series forecasting model. It uses LightGBM with 47 engineered features — lagged values, rolling means, day-of-week dummies, holiday indicators, temperature data. The MAPE is 4.2%, well within business requirements. The model is ready for production. And then your VP of Operations asks: "Why does the model think next Tuesday's demand will spike 18% above the rolling average?" This is the explainability gap. The model knows. You don't — or more precisely, you can't communicate what the model knows in language that a non-technical stakeholder can act on. Raw SHAP values tell you that lag1 contributed +0.32 and rolling7dmean contributed -0.18, but what your VP needs to hear is: "Yesterday's value was unusually high, and the 7-day trend has been climbing, which together push Tuesday's forecast up. The biggest single driver is the previous day's reading." Shapash, the open-source Python library developed by MAIF (a French mutual insurer), bridges this gap. It wraps SHAP and LIME computations in a presentation layer that performs inverse encoding (translating feature17 back into "Neighbourhood: Northridge"), generates interactive dashboards, and produces standalone HTML audit reports. For time series forecasting, the key insight is that Shapash doesn't need to know your data is temporal — it explains the tabular features that your forecasting pipeline produces. The temporal intelligence lives in your feature engineering; Shapash explains the result. Before diving into the implementation, three concepts underpin everything Shapash does. A Shapley value answers: *how much did feature i contribute to this specific prediction, relative to the average prediction? It comes from cooperative game theory (Lloyd Shapley, 1953; Nobel Prize 2012). The idea: consider every possible subset of features ("coalition"). For each, measure how much the prediction changes when you add feature i. The Shapley value is the weighted average of these marginal contributions across all coalitions. It is the only attribution method that simultaneously satisfies local accuracy (contributions sum to the prediction minus the base value), missingness (missing features contribute zero), and consistency (if a feature's marginal contribution increases in a new model, its attribution doesn't decrease). Computing exact Shapley values is exponential in the number of features (2^n^ coalitions). TreeSHAP, by Scott Lundberg (2018), exploits the tree structure of gradient-boosted models to compute exact Shapley values in polynomial time — O(TLD²) where T is the number of trees, L the maximum leaves, and D the maximum depth. This is why Shapash works best with XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost, and scikit-learn tree ensembles: the SHAP backend can compute exact values fast enough for interactive exploration. Most ML pipelines encode categorical features before training. An ordinal encoder maps "Monday" → 0, "Tuesday" → 1, etc. A one-hot encoder creates dowMonday, dowTuesday, ..

docs.title: Safe Ts Article
docs.subtitle: Extracting monotonic, piecewise-constant feature transformations from black-box time series models using ALE profiles and surrogate trees.
docs.body: SAFE: Surrogate-Assisted Feature Extraction How the MI²DataLab method bridges the black-box performance gap for interpretable time series forecasting — and how it compares to seven modern alternatives. 01. The Problem in Time Series Time series forecasting sits at an uncomfortable junction. On one side stands the practitioner deploying an XGBoost model with 200 lag features, a calendar effect matrix, and rolling statistics — achieving 6% MASE on the M4 benchmark but unable to explain to the energy regulator why the model predicts a demand spike at 18:00 on a Wednesday in January. On the other side stands the analyst building a linear regression with hand-crafted features: explainable at every coefficient, but routinely 15–25% worse in forecast accuracy. This is not a new dilemma, but it sharpens in time series for three structural reasons. First, the feature space is inherently high-dimensional: a 30-day lookback window on a daily series with 10 exogenous variables immediately produces 300+ candidate features, and their pairwise interactions — lag₁ × temperature, lag₇ × holidayflag — multiply this combinatorially. Second, the features are autocorrelated by construction: lag₁, lag₂, lag₃ all carry overlapping information, which breaks the independence assumption underpinning Partial Dependence Plots. Third, the data-generating process is non-stationary: a threshold relationship estimated on 2022 data may not hold in 2024 after a regime shift. SAFE's answer to this dilemma is to treat the black-box not as a competitor but as a feature engineer. The black-box learns which regions of the lag and exogenous variable space behave differently. SAFE extracts those regional boundaries — the breakpoints — and re-encodes them as explicit, named indicator features that a linear model can use as coefficients, a regulator can audit, and a domain expert can recognise as meaningful. The canonical paper — Gosiewska, Kozak & Biecek (2021), "Simpler is Better: Lifting the Interpretability-Performance Trade-off via Automated Feature Engineering", published in Decision Support Systems — benchmarks SAFE across 30 tabular datasets and finds that a logistic or linear regression trained on SAFE-transformed features closes 60–85% of the performance gap between a baseline linear model and a black-box, while retaining full coefficient interpretability. The rSAFE R package (Gosiewska, Gierlak, Biecek; CRAN v0.1.4, last updated 2022-08-13) provides the canonical implementation. A Python equivalent, SafeTransformer, mirrors the sklearn API. 02. SAFE Algorithm — Canonical 03rSAFE: The R Reference Implementation The rSAFE package was developed at the MI²DataLab (Warsaw University of Technology) as part of the broader DrWhy.AI ecosystem for responsible machine learning. The canonical demo uses the apartments dataset from DALEX — predicting price per m² from construction year, surface, floor, number of rooms, and district. We annotate this demo fully, as every parameter choice has

docs.title: Rag 2026 Definitive Guide
docs.subtitle: Long-context models can swallow 2 million tokens. Agents can search, verify, and reason in real time. Fine-tuning bakes knowledge into weights. So why are enterprises still building RAG pipelines — and when should the...
docs.body: 1. The Question Everyone Is Asking It's April 2026. Your team has spent six months building a RAG pipeline — chunking 50,000 internal documents, embedding them with OpenAI's text-embedding-3-large, storing them in Pinecone, retrieving top-5 chunks per query, reranking with Cohere Rerank, and generating answers with Claude. The pipeline works. Accuracy is 87% on your evaluation set. Latency is 2.3 seconds. Cost is $0.004 per query. Then your CTO reads an article about Gemini 2.0 with its 2-million-token context window, watches a demo of an AI agent that searches the web and reads documents in real time, and asks: "Why are we maintaining all this infrastructure when the model can just read everything?" It's a fair question. In 2023, when context windows were 8K tokens and models hallucinated constantly, RAG was the only viable architecture for knowledge-grounded AI. In 2026, four paradigm shifts — long-context models, agentic AI, fine-tuning advances, and graph-based retrieval — have fundamentally changed the calculus. RAG is not dead. But it is no longer the default answer to every knowledge-grounding problem. The decision is now: retrieval vs. context stuffing vs. fine-tuning vs. agentic tool-use — and the answer depends on scale, freshness, cost, auditability, and accuracy requirements. This article covers everything: the original RAG formulation and why it was a breakthrough; the paradigm shifts that challenge its relevance; where RAG remains essential and where it's being replaced; the competitive landscape of frameworks and managed services; advanced patterns from Self-RAG to Agentic RAG; evaluation methodology; and an honest decision framework for 2026. Every concept section contains a runnable notebook cell — green-bordered cells 0–9 form a complete working RAG pipeline. 💡 Notebook Map Cells 0–9 woven throughout: Cell 0 Setup (§1) · Cell 1 Chunking (§3) · Cell 2 Embedding + Vector Store (§4) · Cell 3 Retrieval + Reranking (§5) · Cell 4 RAG Chain (§6) · Cell 5 Self-RAG Pattern (§9) · Cell 6 Agentic RAG (§10) · Cell 7 Evaluation (§14) · Cell 8 Long Context Comparison (§7) · Cell 9 Decision Router (§17) The classic RAG pipeline: query → embed → retrieve → rerank → augment prompt → generate → cite. Each stage maps to a notebook cell in this article. 2. The Original RAG: Lewis et al. (NeurIPS 2020) Retrieval-Augmented Generation was formalised by Patrick Lewis, Ethan Perez, and colleagues at Meta AI (FAIR) in their NeurIPS 2020 paper. The core idea: instead of relying solely on the parametric knowledge stored in a language model's weights, augment the generation process with non-parametric memory — a retrieval step that fetches relevant documents from an external corpus. The probabilistic formulation is: p(y|x) = Σz p(y|x,z) · p(z|x), where x is the input query, z is a retrieved document, and y is the generated output. The model marginalises over retrieved documents, combining retrieval probability with generation probability. The original imple

docs.title: Probabilistic Forecasting Guide
docs.subtitle: From "demand will be 215" to "demand will be 215 [195, 238] at 90% coverage." Quantile regression for adaptiveness. Conformal prediction for guarantees. CQR for both. MAPIE for production.
docs.body: 1. Why Point Forecasts Are Not Enough Your demand forecasting model predicts 215 units for next Tuesday. The supply chain team orders 215 units. Actual demand is 238. You stockout, losing $23k in revenue. The next week, the model predicts 220. The team orders 235 "just in case." Actual demand is 198. You overstock, incurring $15k in holding costs. The problem isn't the model's accuracy — it's the absence of uncertainty. A probabilistic forecast — "215 units, 90% prediction interval [195, 238]" — lets the team set safety stock at the 95th percentile, balancing stockout risk against holding cost. This article covers the complete landscape: why point forecasts mislead decision-makers; quantile regression for estimating specific percentiles; conformal prediction for distribution-free coverage guarantees; CQR (Romano et al., NeurIPS 2019) for combining both; MAPIE as the production library; calibration assessment; and four use cases where prediction intervals change the economics. Point forecast (left) gives a single number with no confidence. Probabilistic forecast (right) gives an interval that quantifies uncertainty and enables optimal decision-making. 💡 Notebook Map Cells 0–9: Cell 0 Setup (§1) · Cell 1 Quantile Regression (§3) · Cell 2 Split Conformal (§4) · Cell 3 MAPIE CQR (§5) · Cell 4 Calibration Check (§7) · Cell 5 CV+ / Jackknife+ (§6) · Cell 6 Classification Sets (§9) · Cell 7 Time Series CP (§10) · Cell 8 Visualise Intervals (§8) · Cell 9 Safety Stock (§11) 2. Prediction Intervals vs. Confidence Intervals Practitioners constantly confuse these. A confidence interval quantifies uncertainty about a parameter (e.g., the population mean). A prediction interval quantifies uncertainty about a future observation. Prediction intervals are always wider because they include both parameter uncertainty AND irreducible noise. When the supply chain team asks "what's the range of demand?" they want a prediction interval, not a confidence interval. Using the wrong one leads to systematically narrow intervals and more stockouts than expected. For ML models, the standard prediction interval from linear regression (ŷ ± t·se·√(1 + 1/n + ...)) doesn't apply — it assumes normality, homoscedasticity, and a linear model. ML models violate all three. This is why conformal prediction matters: it provides valid prediction intervals for ANY model with NO distributional assumptions. 3. Quantile Regression Quantile regression directly predicts specific percentiles of the conditional distribution. Instead of minimising mean squared error (which estimates the conditional mean), it minimises the pinball loss: Lτ(y, q) = τ·max(y−q, 0) + (1−τ)·max(q−y, 0), where τ is the target quantile. For τ=0.5, this gives the median. For τ=0.05 and τ=0.95, it gives a 90% prediction interval. The pinball loss is asymmetric: for τ=0.95, under-predictions (y much greater than q) are penalised 19x more than over-predictions. This pushes the model to predict the upper tail. LightGBM, XGBoos

docs.title: Pfi Definitive Guide
docs.subtitle: The simplest, most widely used, and most misunderstood tool in the ML interpretability toolkit. When it works, when it misleads, and what to use instead.
docs.body: 1. The Question PFI Answers Your Random Forest achieves 94% accuracy on the test set using 30 features. The product manager asks: "Which features actually matter?" You need a single, definitive ranking — from most important to least — that works for any model, requires no retraining, and is simple enough to explain to a non-technical stakeholder. This is what Permutation Feature Importance (PFI) provides: for each feature, randomly shuffle its values across the dataset, re-evaluate the model, and measure the performance drop. The larger the drop, the more important the feature. PFI was introduced by Leo Breiman in his seminal 2001 Random Forests paper as an integral part of the RF algorithm. It has since been generalised to work with any model — it's model-agnostic. scikit-learn's permutationimportance (added in v0.22) made it accessible to every Python practitioner. It is now one of the most widely used interpretability tools in production ML. It is also one of the most misunderstood: PFI gives actively misleading results when features are correlated, and most practitioners don't check for this. This article covers everything: the algorithm and its mathematical formulation; the critical multicollinearity trap; train-set vs. test-set PFI; the choice of metric; the scikit-learn implementation; visualisation best practices; four production use cases; the competitive landscape of feature importance methods; and a decision framework. Every concept section contains a runnable notebook cell. 💡 Notebook Map Cells 0–9: Cell 0 Setup (§1) · Cell 1 Basic PFI (§2) · Cell 2 Train vs Test PFI (§3) · Cell 3 Multicollinearity Demo (§5) · Cell 4 Grouped PFI (§6) · Cell 5 Box Plot Visualisation (§8) · Cell 6 Feature Selection Workflow (§9) · Cell 7 Leakage Detection (§10) · Cell 8 PFI vs SHAP (§13) · Cell 9 Multi-Model Comparison (§11) 2. The Core Algorithm The algorithm is elegant in its simplicity. Given a trained model f, a dataset X with features x₁...xₙ, true labels y, and a scoring function L (e.g., accuracy), the permutation importance of feature j is: PFI(j) = L(y, f(X̃ⱼ)) − L(y, f(X)), where X̃ⱼ is the dataset with feature j randomly permuted (shuffled) across rows. The permutation breaks the relationship between feature j and the target while preserving the feature's marginal distribution — no synthetic values are created, just rearranged real values. The five-step PFI algorithm. Compute baseline score, shuffle one feature, re-evaluate, measure the drop, repeat for all features and multiple random seeds. Larger drop = more important. No retraining required. Why this works: if feature j carries information the model uses, shuffling it destroys that information and performance drops. If feature j is irrelevant, shuffling it has no effect. The magnitude of the drop is proportional to how much the model relies on that feature. Because we never retrain the model — only re-evaluate it on perturbed data — PFI is computationally cheap compared to methods that re

docs.title: Feature Selection Ts Deep Dive
docs.subtitle: Mechanics, intuition, diagnostics, and honest limits of 47 methods — with worked examples, inline plots, and how to tell genuine importance from statistical noise.
docs.body: The Practitioner's Deep Dive: Time Series Feature Selection in Practice Mechanics, intuition, diagnostics, and honest limits of all 47 methods — with worked examples, inline plots, interpretation guides, and how to tell genuine importance from statistical noise. The Epistemology of Feature Importance in Time Series There is no ground truth for feature importance in time series. This sentence should be printed above every data scientist's monitor before they run their first feature selector. In cross-sectional data you can generate synthetic data from a known causal structure and verify your selector recovers it. In time series, the causal structure is never fully known, the data-generating process is non-stationary, and measuring importance with a model creates a circular dependency between what you selected and what you measured. What we call "feature importance" is always a model-conditioned, sample-specific, assumption-laden estimate of something we hope approximates true predictive relevance. SHAP values tell you how a particular model uses a feature on a particular test set. Granger p-values tell you whether X's past linearly predicts Y's future conditional on Y's own past, under stationarity assumptions almost never satisfied. PACF spikes tell you which lags of the target's own history carry marginal linear autocorrelation. These methods measure different things — treating any one as "the importance" is the foundational mistake. The practitioner's job is to triangulate a working hypothesis about which features are worth including using multiple lines of imperfect evidence, and to understand specifically how each line of evidence can mislead. This article is a manual for that triangulation, covering all 47 methods in the v2.1 reference with mechanics, diagnostics, and worked examples. 🎯 Operational Definition of Genuine Importance A feature is genuinely important if and only if: (1) including it improves held-out walk-forward CV performance, (2) its measured importance is stable across CV folds and across methods from different families, (3) a plausible mechanistic story explains why it should be predictive, and (4) its importance survives a permutation null test. Meeting all four is rare — which is why most deployed feature sets contain features present by habit or statistical accident. Temporal leakage is the most destructive. Any selector exposed to future information — however indirectly — discovers patterns existing only in the training window. The insidious form is soft leakage: a feature technically observed before prediction time but whose value correlates with future events through a mechanism not available at inference time. Spurious correlation is the second failure mode. In non-stationary time series, any two trending or seasonal variables appear correlated because they share a common driver — time itself. Selectors not accounting for shared autocorrelation rank noise features highly. AR-adjusted partial correlation, PCMCI+, and

docs.title: Ebm Definitive Guide
docs.subtitle: As accurate as gradient boosting, as interpretable as linear regression. Microsoft Research's EBM makes the case that you don't need SHAP if the model explains itself.
docs.body: 1. The Interpretability Question You've trained an XGBoost model for credit scoring. AUC 0.923 on your holdout set. Then compliance asks: "For every denied application, provide the specific features and their exact contributions that led to the denial." You reach for SHAP — compute Shapley values, build a Shapash dashboard, generate an audit report. It works, but it's two systems: the model that decides, and the explanation that rationalises the decision post-hoc. The explanation is approximate, local, and computed after the fact. There is another path. Instead of building a black-box model and explaining it, you build a model that is interpretable by design — one whose internal structure is directly human-readable, where each feature's effect is a curve you can plot, inspect, and edit. And this model matches the black box's accuracy. This is the Explainable Boosting Machine (EBM), developed at Microsoft Research by Rich Caruana and collaborators. It is a tree-based, cyclic gradient boosting Generalised Additive Model with automatic interaction detection. This article covers everything at encyclopaedic depth: the mathematical lineage from GAMs through GA²M to EBM; the round-robin training algorithm; shape functions and their visualisation; the accuracy debate with evidence; the InterpretML library in full; four production use cases; the competitive landscape of glass-box models; model editing; and an honest decision framework. Every concept section contains a runnable notebook cell — Cells 0–9 form a complete end-to-end example. 💡 Notebook Map Cells 0–9: Cell 0 Setup (§1) · Cell 1 Train (§4) · Cell 2 Global Explain (§5) · Cell 3 Local Explain (§5) · Cell 4 Interactions (§6) · Cell 5 Tuning (§8) · Cell 6 Monotonic (§9) · Cell 7 Editing (§10) · Cell 8 vs XGBoost (§12) · Cell 9 Deploy (§16) 2. Generalised Additive Models: The Mathematical Foundation An EBM is a Generalised Additive Model (GAM). GAMs were introduced by Hastie and Tibshirani (1986) as an extension of linear models: g(E[y]) = β₀ + f₁(x₁) + f₂(x₂) + ... + fₙ(xₙ), where g is a link function (identity for regression, logit for classification) and each fᵢ is a univariate "shape function" learned from data. The additive structure is the source of interpretability: each feature contributes independently, so you can visualise fᵢ(xᵢ) as a curve — x-axis is feature value, y-axis is contribution to prediction — and inspect it directly. No post-hoc approximation needed. The lineage: GAMs provide the additive framework. GA²M (Lou et al., KDD 2013) adds pairwise interactions via the FAST algorithm. EBMs add modern ML training — bagging, cyclic gradient boosting, optional differential privacy. The limitation of pure GAMs is accuracy. Because each feature acts independently, GAMs cannot capture interactions — where feature A's effect depends on feature B's value. Lou, Caruana, Gehrke, and Hooker addressed this in their KDD 2013 paper with GA²M: g(E[y]) = β₀ + Σfᵢ(xᵢ) + Σfᵢⱼ(xᵢ,xⱼ). The pairwise terms

docs.title: Conformal Prediction Guide
docs.subtitle: Distribution-free, finite-sample coverage guarantees. No Gaussianity. No Bayesian priors. Works with any model. This is the uncertainty quantification framework that is replacing bootstrap intervals and Bayesian credi...
docs.body: 1. The Uncertainty Problem in ML Your ML model predicts a number. The decision-maker asks: "How confident are you?" The model has no answer. Softmax probabilities are poorly calibrated — a classifier that says "87% confidence" is often wrong 30% of the time. Regression models give point predictions with no error bars. Bootstrap intervals are approximate and tend to undercover. Bayesian methods require specifying priors that are often wrong. The fundamental question — "what is the range of plausible values for this prediction?" — remains unanswered by standard ML. Conformal prediction solves this. It wraps around any model and produces prediction intervals (for regression) or prediction sets (for classification) with a mathematical guarantee: the true value is contained with probability at least 1−α. This guarantee holds for any distribution, any model, with finite samples. The only assumption is exchangeability — a condition weaker than i.i.d. that simply says the ordering of data points doesn't carry information. This article covers everything: the theory, the algorithms, the MAPIE library, and four production use cases. The conformal prediction guarantee. P(Y ∈ C(X)) ≥ 1−α for any distribution, any model, finite samples. Only requires exchangeability. 💡 Notebook Map Cells 0–9: Cell 0 Setup (§1) · Cell 1 Split Conformal (§3) · Cell 2 CQR (§4) · Cell 3 CV+ (§5) · Cell 4 Calibration Check (§7) · Cell 5 Classification Sets (§8) · Cell 6 Visualise (§9) · Cell 7 Compare Methods (§10) · Cell 8 Time Series (§11) · Cell 9 Production (§12) 2. Exchangeability: The Only Assumption Conformal prediction requires one assumption: exchangeability. A sequence of random variables Z₁, Z₂, ..., Zₙ is exchangeable if their joint distribution is invariant under any permutation. This is weaker than i.i.d.: i.i.d. data is always exchangeable, but exchangeable data need not be independent (e.g., sampling without replacement from a finite population is exchangeable but not independent). When does exchangeability fail? Time series — observations are ordered, autocorrelated, and potentially non-stationary. Direct application of conformal prediction to time series gives invalid coverage. Distribution shift — if the test distribution differs from the calibration distribution, the guarantee weakens. Feedback loops — if the model's predictions influence the data-generating process (e.g., a recommendation system), exchangeability breaks. For time series, adaptations exist: EnbPI (Xu & Xie 2021) uses ensemble diversity, ACI (Gibbs & Candès 2021) adjusts online. For distribution shift, robust conformal prediction (Cauchois et al. 2021) and Mondrian conformal provide partial solutions. The key insight: conformal prediction's coverage guarantee degrades gracefully — even when exchangeability is approximately violated, coverage is approximately correct. 3. Split Conformal Prediction The simplest and most practical variant. (1) Split data into training and calibration sets. (2) Train

docs.title: Catboost Guide
docs.subtitle: Ordered boosting eliminates prediction shift. Ordered target statistics handle categoricals without leakage. Symmetric trees regularise and accelerate. Born at Yandex for search ranking, now competing everywhere tabul...
docs.body: 1. The Third Way XGBoost (Chen & Guestrin, KDD 2016) brought gradient boosting to the mainstream. LightGBM (Ke et al., NeurIPS 2017) made it fast with histogram-based splits and leaf-wise growth. CatBoost (Prokhorenkova et al., NeurIPS 2018) made it theoretically principled — identifying and solving a subtle bias (prediction shift) that neither predecessor addressed, while also solving the categorical encoding problem that both left to the user. Gradient boosting algorithms appear in over 80% of winning Kaggle solutions for structured data. The choice between XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost often determines competition rankings and production model quality. This article covers CatBoost's three key innovations — ordered boosting, ordered target statistics for categoricals, and symmetric trees — with honest benchmarks, full code, and a decision framework for when to use each member of the trinity. 💡 Notebook Map Cells 0–9: Cell 0 Setup (§1) · Cell 1 Train CatBoost (§4) · Cell 2 Pool + Cat Features (§5) · Cell 3 Feature Importance (§8) · Cell 4 SHAP Values (§8) · Cell 5 vs XGBoost/LGBM (§9) · Cell 6 GPU Training (§7) · Cell 7 Hyperparameter Tuning (§6) · Cell 8 Ranking (§11) · Cell 9 Deploy (§15) 2. The Prediction Shift Problem Standard gradient boosting has a subtle bias that Prokhorenkova et al. (NeurIPS 2018) were the first to formally identify: prediction shift. At each boosting iteration, trees are built to fit the residuals. But the residuals were computed using the same training data that the trees will be trained on. This means the model has already "seen" the targets when computing residuals — creating a form of conditional label leakage that shifts the gradient estimates and can lead to overfitting, especially on small-to-medium datasets. Standard boosting (left) computes residuals on the same data used for tree building — conditional label leakage. CatBoost's ordered boosting (right) computes each sample's residual using only preceding samples in a random permutation — theoretically unbiased. The bias is small per iteration but accumulates across thousands of boosting rounds. On large datasets with many features, it's negligible — which is why XGBoost and LightGBM work well in practice. On small datasets (under 10K rows) or datasets with many features relative to samples, prediction shift causes real overfitting that tuning alone can't fully fix. CatBoost's ordered boosting eliminates this bias entirely. 3. Ordered Boosting CatBoost's solution: impose a random ordering on the training data. For each sample i, compute its residual using a model trained only on samples 1 through i-1 — never using sample i's own target to compute its own residual. This is equivalent to online learning: each sample is predicted by a model that has never seen it. Multiple random permutations are used for different boosting iterations, providing diversity. The cost: ordered boosting is approximately 1.7x slower than plain boosting (as measured in the NeurIPS 20

docs.title: The Monotonic Constraint Trick That Prevents Nonsensical Predictions
docs.subtitle: How one XGBoost parameter can enforce domain knowledge and eliminate predictions that violate common sense
docs.body: How one XGBoost parameter can enforce domain knowledge and eliminate predictions that violate common sense The Problem You've built a house price model. It's got great RMSE. Then your stakeholder points out: "Why does your model predict a lower price when I add a bedroom?" This happens more often than you'd think. Tree-based models can learn non-monotonic relationships from noisy data — meaning more bedrooms might decrease predicted price in certain feature interaction regions. The model is technically correct (it fits the noise), but it's practically wrong (it violates domain knowledge). Monotonic constraints fix this in one line of code. How Monotonic Constraints Work The constraint tells the model: "I don't care what the data says — the partial dependence of price on bedrooms must NEVER decrease." The model can still learn complex interactions with other features. It just can't violate the monotonic rule for the constrained feature. The Mechanics At every split point, XGBoost checks: "If I split here, will the left child have a higher prediction than the right child for an increasing constraint?" If yes, that split is forbidden. Key Insight: Monotonic constraints don't just clip predictions — they change the tree structure itself. The model learns a fundamentally different (and more sensible) function. Code Example Output: The monotonic model has virtually identical accuracy — but its predictions always make sense. Proof That It Works Output: The unconstrained model has a price drop at 4 bedrooms (marked ↓ !!!). The constrained model is strictly increasing. Constraint Options at a Glance | Value | Meaning | Example Feature | |-------|---------|-----------------| | 1 | Must be increasing | bedrooms → price | | -1 | Must be decreasing | age → price | | 0 | No constraint (default) | color, zip code | Supported Libraries | Library | Parameter | Format | |---------|-----------|--------| | XGBoost | monotoneconstraints | Tuple: (1, 0, -1) | | LightGBM | monotoneconstraints | List: [1, 0, -1] | | CatBoost | monotoneconstraints | Dict: {0: 1, 2: -1} | | Scikit-learn | monotoniccst (v1.4+) | Array: [1, 0, -1] | Key Insight: As of scikit-learn 1.4, HistGradientBoostingRegressor and HistGradientBoostingClassifier also support monotonic constraints via the monotoniccst parameter. You're no longer locked into XGBoost/LightGBM. When to Use This Ideal use cases: Pricing models — price should increase with size, quality, quantity Credit scoring — higher income should never decrease creditworthiness Insurance — more coverage should not decrease premium When NOT to use: Truly non-monotonic relationships — temperature vs. crop yield has a peak Exploratory analysis — you're still discovering relationships in the data When domain knowledge is uncertain — wrong constraints will hurt accuracy TL;DR Cheat Sheet What: Monotonic constraints force tree-based models to respect "more X → more/less Y" rules Why: Prevents nonsensical predictions where adding bedrooms decrea
